quality.ROIC: Defense programs require billions in upfront R&D and facility investment before a single unit ships. ROIC reveals whether those long development cycles actually produce returns above the cost of capital, or whether companies are winning contracts at uneconomic prices.
quality.FCF Margin: Revenue on defense contracts is recognized on a percentage of completion basis, which can look smooth while cash collection is lumpy and milestone driven. FCF Margin cuts through the accrual accounting to show actual cash generation.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Growth in defense comes from winning new program contracts and converting backlog. Rising revenue signals expanding share of defense budgets or successful entry into new platforms.
quality.EBIT Margin: Fixed price contracts are common in defense. Companies that can execute within budget earn high EBIT margins; those that hit cost overruns see margins collapse. This directly measures program execution discipline.

value.EV/EBITDA: Capital structure neutral, which matters because defense companies carry varying levels of debt depending on their acquisition history.
value.P/FCF: Milestone based cash collection means reported earnings and actual cash can diverge significantly. P/FCF shows what you are paying for real cash generation.
value.EV/Sales: Low weight because margins vary widely between cost plus contracts (low margin, low risk) and fixed price contracts (high margin, high risk).
value.P/E: Standard earnings multiple. Works well for mature defense primes with predictable backlog driven revenue.

quality.ROIC: Airlines invest tens of billions in aircraft that fly for 20 to 30 years. A fleet decision (buying 737 MAXs vs A320neos, owning vs leasing) locks in returns for decades. ROIC shows which airlines deploy fleet capital efficiently and which are destroying value.
quality.FCF Margin: An airline can report positive EBITDA while burning cash on aircraft deliveries, engine overhauls, and debt service. FCF Margin reveals the cash actually available after these massive obligations, which EBITDA hides.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Revenue per available seat mile (RASM) is the industry pulse. Growth comes from filling more seats, raising fares, or expanding routes. Flat or declining revenue in a growing travel market signals competitive weakness.
quality.EBITDA Margin: Depreciation varies wildly depending on fleet age and whether planes are owned or leased. A carrier with new planes has high depreciation but low maintenance; an older fleet has the opposite. EBITDA normalizes these differences.

value.EV/EBITDA: Enterprise value captures the massive operating lease obligations that airlines carry. Using EV instead of market cap gives a truer picture of total cost to acquire the business.
value.P/FCF: Airlines can report strong EBITDA while burning cash on fleet renewal. P/FCF is the reality check that shows what investors actually receive after the planes are paid for.
value.EV/Sales: Low weight because airlines are a high volume, thin margin business. Revenue alone tells you little about profitability.
value.P/E: Volatile because fuel hedging gains and losses, fleet impairments, and one time items swing earnings quarter to quarter.

quality.Gross Margin: Lower weight because fee based businesses inherently have high gross margins. It is less differentiating here than in manufacturing or retail.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Revenue is a direct function of assets under management. Growth signals organic inflows (clients choosing your funds) rather than just market appreciation lifting AUM.
quality.ROE: Asset managers require almost no physical capital. With such a light asset base, ROE should be exceptional. A low ROE means the firm is either overpaying for talent, losing clients, or has a bloated cost structure.
quality.ROA: Captures efficiency across the full balance sheet including any proprietary investments the firm holds.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard but lower weight. Fee income is straightforward to value.
value.P/FCF: Lower weight because cash conversion is naturally clean for fee based businesses with no inventory or physical assets.
value.P/E: Highest weight. Management fee earnings are the primary valuation driver. Stable, recurring fee income makes P/E reliable.
value.Price to Book: Represents accumulated capital and the value of the client franchise.

quality.ROIC: The EV transition is forcing automakers to invest billions retooling factories. ROIC shows whether those investments are generating returns, or whether companies are spending to stay relevant without earning an adequate return.
quality.Gross Margin: Separates premium automakers (Porsche, Ferrari) from volume manufacturers (GM, Ford). A premium brand can price above cost; a volume player competes on efficiency.
quality.FCF Margin: Massive retooling capex for EVs and new platforms means cash outflows can dwarf reported earnings. FCF Margin reveals the true cost of the transition.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Tracks unit volume and pricing trends. In a cyclical industry, revenue growth during a downturn signals market share gains.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard for capital intensive manufacturing businesses.
value.P/FCF: Capex is enormous and variable. P/FCF reveals what investors receive after billions in factory and tooling investment.
value.EV/Sales: Spans a wide margin spectrum from OEMs to parts suppliers to aftermarket companies.
value.P/E: Standard but cyclical. Earnings at the peak of the auto cycle are not sustainable.

quality.Revenue YoY %: Driven by loan growth and net interest income. Rising revenue signals expanding loan books and healthy credit demand.
quality.ROE: The defining bank metric. ROIC is meaningless here because a bank's 'debt' is customer deposits, which is actually the raw material of the business, not leverage. ROE measures how well the bank deploys shareholder equity to generate returns.
quality.ROA: Measures how efficiently the bank earns returns across its entire loan and securities portfolio. Lower weight because total assets include low yielding required reserves.
quality.Net Inc. Margin: Captures the full picture: net interest income plus fee income minus provisions for loan losses and operating expenses.

value.P/E: 50% weight. Earnings smooth out quarterly provision volatility and capture the combined result of lending, fee income, and credit losses.
value.Price to Book: 50% weight. Book value represents the equity capital supporting the loan portfolio. EV multiples like EV/EBITDA are excluded entirely because bank debt is deposits (customer money held in trust), not borrowing. Enterprise value is meaningless when deposits make up 80%+ of liabilities.

quality.ROIC: Lowest weight (10%) because most biotechs are burning cash by design, investing in R&D pipelines before any drug reaches market. But it still rewards the rare biotech that has crossed into profitability.
quality.Gross Margin: Highest weight (35%). For biotechs with commercial products, gross margin reflects drug pricing power and manufacturing efficiency. A biotech selling a drug at negative gross margin has a fundamental problem with its product economics.
quality.FCF Margin: Cash burn rate determines survival. A biotech with $500M in cash burning $200M per year has 2.5 years of runway. FCF Margin quantifies how efficiently the company converts revenue to cash or conserves its reserves.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Highest weight (35%). For biotechs, revenue growth is proof the pipeline is translating into commercial success. The jump from zero to first revenue is the most transformational event in a biotech's life.

value.EV/Sales: 50% weight. The only reliable valuation tool for this group because most biotechs have no earnings, no EBITDA, and no free cash flow. Revenue multiples are the sole basis for comparison.
value.P/FCF: Important for the few commercial stage biotechs generating cash. Irrelevant for pre revenue companies where FCF is deeply negative.
value.EV/EBITDA: Lower weight (20%) because most biotechs have negative EBITDA. The metric only applies to the minority that have reached operating profitability.

quality.ROIC: Quarries and cement plants require enormous upfront capital and operate for decades. ROIC shows whether these long lived assets are earning above their cost of capital.
quality.FCF Margin: Plants require constant heavy maintenance capex to stay operational. FCF Margin reveals cash available after this non discretionary spending.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Lower weight because revenue is partly driven by commodity pricing (aggregates, cement) which the company cannot control.
quality.EBITDA Margin: Used instead of Gross Margin because transportation costs and energy are the key differentiators between building materials companies, and those fall below the gross profit line.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard for asset heavy industries with large property and equipment bases.
value.P/FCF: Maintenance capex on plants and quarries is non discretionary, creating a meaningful gap between EBITDA and free cash flow.
value.EV/Sales: Spans from commodity aggregates to specialty products with very different margin profiles.
value.P/E: Lower weight because depletion accounting (similar to depreciation but for quarry reserves) distorts reported earnings.

quality.ROIC: Manufacturing facilities and brand building both require capital. ROIC measures whether these investments generate adequate returns.
quality.Gross Margin: Separates branded, differentiated products (higher margins) from commodity building supplies (thin margins).
quality.FCF Margin: Construction activity is seasonal, creating cash flow swings. FCF Margin shows the full year cash picture after these cycles.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Driven by housing starts, renovation activity, and share gains. Growth signals positioning in the right end markets.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard for manufacturing businesses.
value.P/FCF: Capex is variable across product cycles and capacity expansions.
value.EV/Sales: Spans a wide margin spectrum across different product categories.
value.P/E: Standard earnings multiple for this group.

quality.ROIC: Media companies invest billions in content libraries, studio facilities, and distribution networks. ROIC reveals whether those bets are actually generating returns or whether companies are overspending on programming that does not attract enough subscribers to justify the cost.
quality.FCF Margin: Content spending is front loaded (you pay to make a show before anyone watches it), but revenue trickles in over years. FCF Margin shows actual cash after this timing mismatch, cutting through content amortization schedules.
quality.Revenue YoY %: The streaming transition is creating clear winners and losers. Revenue growth separates companies gaining subscribers from those losing them to competitors.
quality.EBITDA Margin: Highest weight (30%). Used instead of net income because content amortization schedules are accounting choices that vary by company. EBITDA strips this out and compares underlying operational performance.

value.EV/EBITDA: The media industry standard. Allows comparison across companies with different content capitalization policies.
value.P/FCF: Content capex creates a large gap between EBITDA and actual cash. P/FCF is the reality check.
value.EV/Sales: Low weight because margin profiles differ dramatically between advertising supported, subscription, and hybrid models.
value.P/E: Standard but content write downs and restructuring charges frequently distort reported earnings.

quality.FCF Margin: REITs typically report FFO (Funds From Operations), but yFinance does not provide FFO. FCF Margin serves as the closest proxy, capturing cash available after maintenance capex on properties.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Driven by lease spreads on renewals and occupancy rates. Growing revenue means rents are rising and spaces are filling.
quality.ROA: Measures how efficiently the property portfolio generates returns across diverse property types.
quality.EBITDA Margin: Separates net lease REITs (high margins, tenant pays expenses) from traditional landlords (lower margins, landlord bears costs).

value.EV/EBITDA: REIT industry standard for comparing across capital structures.
value.P/FCF: Directly tied to dividend sustainability. REITs must distribute 90% of taxable income.
value.P/E: Low weight because heavy depreciation on buildings distorts REIT earnings. P/E understates true profitability.
value.Price to Book: Particularly important post COVID for office REITs where the market is questioning whether book value reflects real property worth.

quality.ROIC: Labs, testing equipment, and clinical trial facilities require significant capital. ROIC shows whether these investments produce adequate returns.
quality.Gross Margin: Separates specialized, high value testing (genetic sequencing, rare disease) from commoditized routine diagnostics.
quality.FCF Margin: Contract research revenues are recognized on milestones, but cash collection follows a different schedule. FCF Margin reveals actual cash generation.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Driven by new test approvals, backlog conversion, and outsourcing trends in pharma R&D.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard for facility based healthcare businesses.
value.P/FCF: Milestone timing means cash often diverges from reported earnings.
value.EV/Sales: Spans a wide margin spectrum from high value specialized diagnostics to routine testing.
value.P/E: Standard earnings multiple for this group.

quality.FCF Margin: Cell towers and data centers report FFO, but yFinance does not provide FFO. FCF Margin serves as the closest proxy, measuring cash after the heavy maintenance capex these assets require.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Driven by 5G deployments, cloud computing growth, and AI infrastructure demand. These are secular tailwinds that should drive steady growth.
quality.ROA: These businesses sit on massive asset bases (towers, data centers). ROA measures whether that infrastructure generates adequate returns on its enormous cost.
quality.EBITDA Margin: Tower companies and data centers operate as toll booths: 60 to 80% EBITDA margins are the defining characteristic. Lower margins signal operational problems.

value.EV/EBITDA: Infrastructure standard. Captures the debt used to finance tower and data center construction.
value.P/FCF: New tower builds and data center expansion create a significant gap between EBITDA and free cash flow.
value.P/E: Low weight because heavy depreciation on infrastructure assets distorts reported earnings significantly.
value.Price to Book: Measures the premium the market places on infrastructure above replacement cost.

quality.Revenue YoY %: For utilities, revenue growth comes from regulators approving rate base increases. Growing revenue signals successful rate cases and expanding infrastructure investment.
quality.ROE: The single most important utility metric. Regulators explicitly set the allowed return on equity, typically 9 to 11%. A utility consistently earning near or above its allowed ROE is executing well.
quality.ROA: Measures efficiency across the massive regulated infrastructure base.
quality.EBIT Margin: EBIT is used instead of EBITDA because depreciation is a real, significant cost for utilities with billions in physical grid infrastructure that degrades and must be replaced.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard for capital intensive regulated businesses.
value.P/FCF: Massive ongoing grid investment means utilities rarely generate free cash flow. P/FCF shows the gap between earnings and actual cash after grid spend.
value.P/E: Regulated earnings are predictable, making P/E a reliable valuation metric for utilities.
value.Price to Book: Serves as a proxy for the regulated rate base. A utility trading below book value suggests the market doubts its ability to earn its allowed return.

quality.ROIC: Project based businesses invest capital in equipment, working capital, and performance bonds. ROIC reveals which firms earn adequate returns on this capital and which win contracts at uneconomic bids.
quality.FCF Margin: Revenue is recognized on percentage of completion, but cash collection often lags by months or years. FCF Margin shows the real cash picture.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Measures backlog conversion and new contract wins. Sustained growth signals a healthy pipeline of projects.
quality.EBIT Margin: The purest measure of execution discipline on fixed price contracts. Cost overruns show up immediately in EBIT margin.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard for project based businesses.
value.P/FCF: The gap between revenue recognition and cash collection is often large on multi year projects.
value.EV/Sales: Low weight because margin varies significantly by contract type (cost plus vs fixed price).
value.P/E: Standard earnings multiple.

quality.ROIC: Brand building and distribution networks require sustained investment. ROIC measures whether decades of brand spending translate into durable competitive returns.
quality.Gross Margin: The most revealing metric in this group. Separates brand owners like Coca Cola (60% gross margin) from distributors like Sysco (18%). High gross margin means pricing power; low means you compete on logistics.
quality.FCF Margin: Mature consumer brands should convert earnings to cash cleanly. If FCF is significantly below net income, something is wrong with working capital management.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Organic revenue growth (excluding M&A) signals brand health and pricing power. Declining organic growth means consumers are switching to competitors or private label.

value.EV/EBITDA: Consumer staples standard for comparing across companies.
value.P/FCF: These are cash generating machines. P/FCF is the most honest measure of what you pay for that cash stream.
value.EV/Sales: Spans a wide spectrum from brand owners (high margin) to distributors (thin margin).
value.P/E: Standard for predictable consumer earnings that grow steadily.

quality.ROIC: Hardware companies carry significant inventory and manufacturing assets. ROIC reveals who earns adequate returns on these investments and who is stuck in low return commodity hardware.
quality.Gross Margin: The defining differentiator. Apple earns 45% gross margin on differentiated products; Dell earns 22% competing on efficiency. High gross margin means customers pay for design, ecosystem, or brand rather than just specs.
quality.FCF Margin: Inventory builds can trap enormous amounts of cash. A company reporting strong earnings while inventory balloons has a problem that FCF Margin exposes.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Hardware goes through product refresh cycles. Companies positioned on the right side of a cycle (new product launches, technology transitions) grow; those on the wrong side stagnate.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard for hardware manufacturers.
value.P/FCF: Catches the inventory cash trap. A company with rising P/FCF relative to P/E may be building inventory that will never sell.
value.EV/Sales: Spans a wide margin spectrum from premium consumer hardware to commodity networking equipment.
value.P/E: Standard earnings multiple.

quality.ROIC: Healthcare facilities (hospitals, clinics, labs) require significant capital investment. ROIC shows whether those facilities earn above their cost of capital.
quality.FCF Margin: Thin margin business where working capital discipline matters enormously. Slow insurance reimbursement collection can turn a profitable company cash negative.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Driven by patient volumes, payer mix, and reimbursement rate changes. Growth signals either market share gains or favorable demographic trends.
quality.EBIT Margin: Used instead of Gross Margin because labor is the dominant cost and falls below the gross profit line. EBIT captures the full cost picture including physician and nursing expenses.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard for facility based healthcare businesses.
value.P/FCF: Heavy maintenance capex on medical facilities and equipment creates a gap between EBITDA and cash.
value.EV/Sales: Low weight. Margin varies significantly between hospital systems, outpatient clinics, and lab services.
value.P/E: Standard earnings multiple.

quality.ROIC: The land bank is a homebuilder's largest investment. ROIC measures how efficiently builders turn land inventory into homes and returns, and how well they time land purchases across the housing cycle.
quality.FCF Margin: Homebuilders must constantly replenish land inventory. FCF Margin after land purchases reveals the true cash available to shareholders, which is often far less than reported net income.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Driven by home closings, pricing, and backlog conversion. Revenue growth signals demand strength and successful community openings.
quality.Net Inc. Margin: Used instead of EBITDA because land impairments (writing down land bought at the wrong price) are a real and important cost. EBITDA would hide this risk.

value.EV/EBITDA: Low weight because EBITDA misses land impairments, which are the biggest risk in homebuilding.
value.P/FCF: Land purchases are lumpy and variable, making FCF volatile. Important but must be interpreted over full cycles.
value.P/E: Standard earnings multiple.
value.Price to Book: Highest weight (40%). The land bank is the core asset. Price to Book tells you what the market thinks the land is worth relative to what the builder paid for it.

quality.ROIC: Hotels invest in properties, gaming companies in casinos, and leisure companies in attractions. ROIC shows whether these capital intensive assets generate returns that justify the investment.
quality.FCF Margin: Properties require constant renovation (hotels refresh rooms every 5 to 7 years, casinos update floors). FCF Margin reveals true profit after this non discretionary maintenance spending.
quality.Revenue YoY %: RevPAR (revenue per available room) is the industry pulse. Growth signals pricing power, occupancy recovery, or successful expansion into new markets.
quality.EBITDA Margin: Highest weight (30%). The hospitality industry standard. Depreciation varies by property age and ownership model (owned vs managed vs franchised), so EBITDA is the common comparison metric.

value.EV/EBITDA: Highest weight (40%). The industry standard valuation metric for hospitality.
value.P/FCF: Renovation capex creates a meaningful gap between EBITDA and actual cash available to shareholders.
value.EV/Sales: Low weight because the business model (asset light franchise vs owned properties) dramatically affects revenue recognition.
value.P/E: Lower weight because depreciation varies significantly by ownership model.

quality.ROIC: These companies invest in brands, marketing, and distribution rather than factories. With such a light asset base, ROIC should be exceptional. Low ROIC signals a brand losing its premium.
quality.Gross Margin: Directly measures brand pricing power. A premium brand like Estee Lauder or Procter & Gamble should command 60%+ gross margins. Declining gross margin means consumers are trading down to cheaper alternatives.
quality.FCF Margin: Mature, established brands should generate significant distributable cash. Weak FCF suggests either over investment in failing new products or poor working capital management.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Organic revenue growth signals brand health. In a low growth category, any organic growth means you are taking share from competitors.

value.EV/EBITDA: Consumer staples standard.
value.P/FCF: Primary for consistent cash generators. These are yield investments valued on cash distributions.
value.EV/Sales: Spans a margin spectrum across beauty, personal care, and household products.
value.P/E: Standard for predictable consumer earnings.

quality.ROIC: IT services companies need minimal physical capital (no factories, minimal inventory). With so little capital required, ROIC should be high. Low ROIC means the firm is overpaying for talent or winning contracts at poor economics.
quality.Gross Margin: Measures billing rate vs labor cost economics. A company billing $200/hour for consultants costing $100/hour has strong gross margin; one billing $120/hour for $100/hour consultants is in trouble.
quality.FCF Margin: Service businesses should convert earnings to cash cleanly because there is no inventory or heavy capex. A gap between net income and FCF signals problematic working capital or aggressive revenue recognition.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Measures contract pipeline health and deal flow. Growth signals either new client wins or expanding scope with existing clients.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard for service businesses.
value.P/FCF: Confirms clean cash conversion. If P/FCF is much higher than P/E, something is wrong with cash collection.
value.EV/Sales: Compares across a wide margin spectrum from high value consulting to commodity outsourcing.
value.P/E: Reliable because contracted backlogs make earnings relatively predictable.

quality.ROIC: Distribution is an inventory intensive business. ROIC measures how efficiently companies turn inventory (buying, warehousing, and delivering products) into returns. Fast inventory turns drive high ROIC.
quality.Gross Margin: Separates value added distributors (offering technical support, custom kitting, logistics) from commodity distributors competing purely on price.
quality.FCF Margin: Inventory ties up enormous amounts of cash. FCF Margin reveals how well the company manages this, and whether growing revenue is actually producing cash or just growing the warehouse.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Growth signals expanding share of customer wallet and geographic expansion.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard for distribution businesses.
value.P/FCF: Inventory cash requirements create a meaningful gap between earnings and cash.
value.EV/Sales: Higher weight because scale is the primary differentiator in distribution. Larger distributors negotiate better supplier terms.
value.P/E: Standard earnings multiple.

quality.ROIC: Machinery companies build a large installed base over time. The aftermarket (spare parts, service contracts) earned on that base should drive increasingly high returns. Rising ROIC over time signals a growing, profitable installed base.
quality.Gross Margin: Separates companies selling differentiated, engineered products (high margin) from those selling commoditized equipment (thin margin).
quality.FCF Margin: Large order backlogs mean working capital cycles can be long. FCF Margin shows whether the company manages these cycles to actually generate cash.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Order intake and backlog conversion. Growing revenue signals healthy industrial demand and successful new product introductions.

value.EV/EBITDA: Manufacturing industry standard.
value.P/FCF: Capex is variable as companies invest in new product lines or expand capacity.
value.EV/Sales: Spans from low margin systems to high margin aftermarket businesses.
value.P/E: Standard earnings multiple.

quality.FCF Margin: REITs report FFO (Funds From Operations), but yFinance does not provide FFO. FCF Margin is used as the closest proxy, capturing cash after maintenance capex on warehouse and logistics properties.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Measures net operating income growth from rent increases, occupancy gains, and new property acquisitions.
quality.ROA: Industrial REITs sit on large property portfolios. ROA measures how efficiently each dollar of property generates rental income.
quality.EBITDA Margin: Captures operating performance before depreciation, which is a non cash accounting charge on long lived industrial properties.

value.EV/EBITDA: REIT industry standard.
value.P/FCF: Highest weight (35%). Directly tied to dividend sustainability since REITs must distribute 90% of taxable income.
value.P/E: Low weight because heavy depreciation on buildings makes P/E artificially high for REITs.
value.Price to Book: Measures the market's view of what the industrial properties are worth relative to their accounting cost.

quality.Revenue YoY %: Premium growth from rate increases, new policies, and market expansion. Rising premiums after a hard market cycle signal pricing discipline.
quality.ROE: The primary insurance return metric. Shareholders provide equity capital that supports the underwriting risk pool. ROE measures how well the insurer puts that capital to work through underwriting profit and investment income.
quality.ROA: Lower weight because investment portfolios (bonds, stocks held from float) inflate total assets. A large investment portfolio makes ROA look low even for well run insurers.
quality.Net Inc. Margin: Captures the complete picture: underwriting profit from premiums, investment income from the float, minus claims paid and operating expenses.

value.P/E: 50% weight. Earnings smooth out the natural volatility of insurance claims. One bad hurricane quarter should not define valuation.
value.Price to Book: 50% weight. Book value represents the equity capital supporting the underwriting portfolio. EV multiples are excluded because policyholder reserves (premiums collected but not yet paid as claims) are liabilities on the balance sheet but are actually the business model, not debt.

quality.ROIC: Internet platforms are built once and scaled globally with near zero marginal cost. This should produce exceptional returns on invested capital. Low ROIC for a scaled platform signals either massive overspending on content or a business model that does not scale as expected.
quality.Gross Margin: Highest weight (30%). Platforms must sustain 60%+ gross margins to justify their valuations. Below that, the platform is spending too much on content, hosting, or moderation relative to revenue.
quality.FCF Margin: Provides a clean view of cash generation, avoiding GAAP noise from content amortization, stock compensation accounting, and restructuring charges.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Winner take all dynamics dominate internet markets. Rapid growth signals network effects compounding; slowing growth may signal the platform is losing relevance.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard but many platforms have negative EBITDA from growth spending.
value.P/FCF: Lower weight because stock based compensation inflates free cash flow by substituting equity dilution for cash compensation expense.
value.EV/Sales: Higher weight (30%). The primary valuation tool for high growth platforms that have not yet reached EBITDA profitability.
value.PEG Ratio: Growth rates vary enormously in this group (0% to 70%+). PEG normalizes P/E for growth, preventing fast growers from looking expensive and slow growers from looking cheap on P/E alone.

quality.ROIC: Pharma companies invest billions in R&D pipelines and acquisitions. ROIC measures whether those bets (which can take 10+ years to pay off) ultimately produce returns above the cost of capital.
quality.Gross Margin: Patented drugs should produce 70 to 85% gross margins, reflecting the pricing power that comes from patent protection. Declining gross margin signals generic competition or loss of exclusivity.
quality.FCF Margin: Highest weight (30%). Cash funds the R&D pipeline and acquisitions. GAAP earnings are distorted by amortization of acquired intangibles, so FCF is the honest measure of profitability.
quality.Revenue YoY %: The constant tension between new drug launches (growth) and patent cliffs (decline). Sustained revenue growth signals a productive pipeline replacing lost exclusivity.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard pharma valuation multiple.
value.P/FCF: Preferred over P/E because acquisitive pharma companies carry massive intangible amortization that depresses reported earnings without affecting cash.
value.EV/Sales: Compares diversified mega pharma (lower growth, multiple drugs) to focused specialty pharma (higher growth, fewer drugs).
value.P/E: Standard but heavily distorted by acquisition amortization for companies that have done large M&A.

quality.ROIC: Health insurers are capital light businesses. With minimal physical assets, ROIC should be high. Low ROIC suggests poor underwriting discipline or excessive administrative costs.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Driven by membership growth, premium increases, and expansion into new plans (Medicare Advantage, Medicaid). Growth signals market share gains.
quality.ROE: Used instead of Gross Margin because insurance has no traditional COGS concept. Premium revenue minus medical costs is the 'margin' but it does not map to standard gross profit accounting.
quality.Net Inc. Margin: Even small margin improvements are significant when applied to massive premium revenue bases. A 0.5% improvement at UnitedHealth means over $1.5B in additional profit.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard but lower weight for managed care.
value.P/FCF: Lower weight because medical reserve adjustments create lumpy cash flows that do not reflect underlying business performance.
value.EV/Sales: Low weight. Margins are thin (3 to 5%) making revenue a poor proxy for value.
value.P/E: Highest weight (40%). Managed care earnings are predictable because large enrolled populations provide actuarial stability.

quality.ROIC: Device companies invest in R&D, manufacturing, and regulatory approvals. ROIC measures whether these multi year investments produce returns, separating innovative device makers from commodity manufacturers.
quality.Gross Margin: Reflects the regulatory moat. FDA approved devices with strong clinical evidence command premium pricing; commodity devices compete on cost alone.
quality.FCF Margin: Separates organic cash generation from acquisition driven growth. Many device companies grow through M&A; FCF shows the cash the core business produces.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Driven by new product launches, procedure volume growth, and adoption of new technologies (robotic surgery, AI diagnostics).

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard for healthcare manufacturers.
value.P/FCF: Preferred over P/E because acquisitive device companies carry large intangible amortization charges that depress reported earnings.
value.EV/Sales: Spans a wide spectrum from differentiated surgical devices (high margin) to commodity supplies (low margin).
value.P/E: Standard but distorted by acquisition amortization for serial acquirers.

quality.ROIC: Measures cost curve position. Miners at the low end of the cost curve earn positive ROIC even when commodity prices fall. High cost miners destroy capital in downturns.
quality.FCF Margin: Mining requires massive development capex (opening new mines, expanding pits). FCF Margin shows cash available after this spending, which EBITDA completely hides.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Lower weight (20%) because revenue is largely driven by commodity prices that the company cannot control.
quality.EBITDA Margin: Measures mine operating efficiency. The costs a miner can control (energy, labor, processing) determine EBITDA margin at any given commodity price.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard for cyclical commodity businesses.
value.P/FCF: Highest weight (35%). The primary mining valuation metric because mine development capex is enormous and non discretionary.
value.EV/Sales: Low weight. Revenue is almost entirely commodity price dependent and uninformative about the quality of the operation.
value.P/E: Volatile because commodity price swings and asset impairments create wild earnings swings.

quality.ROIC: Drilling wells costs millions each. ROIC measures finding and development cost efficiency, separating companies drilling profitable wells from those wasting capital on marginal acreage.
quality.FCF Margin: Highest weight (30%). Cash generation is the entire investor thesis for E&P companies. Investors want to see cash returned via dividends and buybacks, not reinvested in unprofitable drilling.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Lowest weight (15%) because revenue is primarily driven by oil and gas prices, which the company cannot control.
quality.EBITDA Margin: Upstream operating efficiency standard. Measures how much of each revenue dollar is left after lifting costs, transportation, and operating expenses.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard upstream valuation multiple.
value.P/FCF: Highest weight (40%). FCF yield is the metric E&P investors focus on. It measures how quickly you get paid back relative to the stock price.
value.EV/Sales: Low weight because revenue is commodity price dependent.
value.P/E: Low weight. Depletion, depreciation, and amortization (DD&A) distort earnings, and commodity volatility makes any single period's earnings unreliable.

quality.ROIC: Refinery complexity (ability to process different crude types) and crude slate flexibility determine returns. Complex refineries earn more because they can buy cheaper heavy crude and produce high value products.
quality.FCF Margin: Refineries require periodic turnarounds (complete shutdowns for maintenance) every 3 to 5 years costing hundreds of millions. FCF Margin reveals cash available after these lumpy but mandatory expenses.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Driven by throughput volumes and capacity utilization. Growing revenue signals operational reliability and favorable crack spreads.
quality.EBITDA Margin: Measures refining efficiency: how much margin the company earns on each barrel processed.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard for asset heavy refining businesses.
value.P/FCF: Turnaround maintenance creates enormous gaps between EBITDA and actual cash in turnaround years.
value.EV/Sales: Low weight. Revenue is misleading because it includes the full cost of crude oil passed through, not just the refining margin earned.
value.P/E: Crack spread volatility (the difference between crude cost and refined product prices) makes earnings swing wildly.

quality.ROIC: Separates technology driven service companies (directional drilling, reservoir analytics) from commodity service providers (basic well maintenance). Technology companies earn higher returns on their equipment investment.
quality.FCF Margin: Service fleets (rigs, pressure pumping equipment, wireline units) require constant maintenance and periodic replacement. FCF Margin shows cash after these non discretionary costs.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Directly tied to rig count and upstream drilling activity. Rising revenue signals increasing demand from E&P companies.
quality.EBITDA Margin: Service efficiency and pricing power. In tight markets, service companies can raise day rates; in oversupply, rates collapse.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard for service businesses.
value.P/FCF: Fleet capex creates a meaningful gap between EBITDA and actual cash.
value.EV/Sales: Technology service providers earn much higher margins than commodity services.
value.P/E: Lower weight because cyclical earnings make any single period unreliable.

quality.ROIC: Payment networks operate as toll booths on global commerce, taking a small fee on every transaction. This model should produce exceptionally high returns on invested capital because marginal costs approach zero.
quality.Gross Margin: Separates network owners like Visa and Mastercard (75%+ gross margin, they set the rules) from payment processors like Fiserv and FIS (45%, they execute transactions for others).
quality.FCF Margin: Capital light processors should convert nearly all earnings to cash. A gap between net income and FCF signals either aggressive revenue recognition or hidden investment needs.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Driven by the secular shift from cash to digital payments and GDP growth increasing total transaction volume.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard for payment networks and processors.
value.P/FCF: Clean cash conversion expected for these capital light businesses.
value.EV/Sales: Spans a wide margin spectrum from network owners to merchant acquirers.
value.P/E: Standard for predictable, transaction based earnings.

quality.ROIC: Lower weight (20%) because midstream assets are legacy infrastructure with known return profiles. New projects must clear hurdle rates, but the existing asset base is mature.
quality.FCF Margin: Highest weight (30%). Distributable cash flow is the entire investor thesis. Midstream investors buy these stocks for yield, and FCF funds the distributions.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Lowest weight (15%) because revenue is largely contracted with take or pay agreements. Growth is slow and predictable by design.
quality.EBITDA Margin: Highest weight (35%). Pipelines and processing plants are toll booth businesses. EBITDA margin measures the efficiency of these operations.

value.EV/EBITDA: Highest weight (40%). The midstream industry standard because it captures the debt used to finance pipeline construction.
value.P/FCF: 35% weight. Cash yield is the investor thesis. Most midstream investors care about distribution coverage and growth.
value.EV/Sales: Low weight because contracted revenue is already predictable.
value.P/E: Low weight because heavy depreciation on pipeline infrastructure distorts reported earnings.

quality.ROIC: Highest weight (35%). Railroad networks are irreplaceable infrastructure (no new Class I railroads will be built). These assets must earn their cost of capital because the capital deployed is enormous and permanent.
quality.FCF Margin: Railroads must constantly reinvest in track, bridges, and rolling stock just to maintain the network. FCF Margin shows what is left after this non discretionary spending.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Lowest weight (15%) because railroad revenue is mature and GDP linked. Meaningful above GDP growth is rare.
quality.EBIT Margin: Operating ratio (100% minus EBIT margin) is the primary management KPI for railroads. A 60% operating ratio means 40 cents of every revenue dollar reaches operating income.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard for infrastructure businesses.
value.P/FCF: The large gap between EBITDA and FCF (from constant maintenance capex) makes P/FCF essential. EBITDA overstates true earnings for railroads.
value.EV/Sales: Low weight because revenue is mature and stable.
value.P/E: Standard for predictable infrastructure earnings.

quality.Revenue YoY %: Growth comes from regulators approving rate increases to fund grid investment. Revenue growth signals successful rate cases and expanding infrastructure programs.
quality.ROE: Regulators set the allowed return on equity, making ROE the defining performance metric. Consistently earning the allowed ROE means the utility is executing on rate cases and managing costs.
quality.ROA: Measures asset efficiency across the regulated generation, transmission, and distribution infrastructure.
quality.EBIT Margin: EBIT is used instead of EBITDA because depreciation is a real and large cost for utilities with massive physical infrastructure. Ignoring depreciation would overstate profitability.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard for capital intensive regulated businesses.
value.P/FCF: After funding grid investment, actual free cash flow determines dividend sustainability.
value.P/E: Predictable regulated earnings make P/E a reliable metric.
value.Price to Book: Acts as a proxy for the regulated rate base value.

quality.ROIC: Renewable projects (solar farms, wind installations) require enormous upfront investment. ROIC measures whether the contracted energy output produces returns above the cost of building and financing these projects.
quality.FCF Margin: Project development investment consumes cash for years before generating returns. FCF Margin shows actual cash after this development spend.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Secular adoption of clean energy should drive consistent growth. Slowing growth would signal either market saturation or policy headwinds.
quality.EBITDA Margin: Highest weight (30%). Measures project level operating efficiency before depreciation on long lived assets.

value.EV/EBITDA: Highest weight (40%). Infrastructure standard for capital intensive energy projects.
value.P/FCF: 35% weight. Project development capex creates an enormous gap between EBITDA and actual free cash flow.
value.EV/Sales: 25% weight. Many renewable companies are still pre EBITDA, making revenue the only basis for comparison.

quality.FCF Margin: REITs report FFO (Funds From Operations), but yFinance does not provide FFO. FCF Margin is the closest available proxy, measuring cash after property maintenance and renovation costs.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Driven by rent increases, occupancy rates, and same store NOI growth. Rising rents in a tight housing market signal pricing power.
quality.ROA: Measures how efficiently the residential property portfolio generates rental income per dollar of property assets.
quality.EBITDA Margin: Pre depreciation operating performance on long lived apartment and housing assets.

value.EV/EBITDA: REIT industry standard.
value.P/FCF: Highest weight (35%). Directly tied to dividend sustainability since REITs must distribute 90% of taxable income.
value.P/E: Low weight because heavy depreciation on buildings distorts REIT earnings.
value.Price to Book: Measures the market's valuation of the residential property portfolio relative to cost.

quality.ROIC: Highest weight (30%). The franchise vs owned decision is the defining strategic question in restaurants. A franchise model (McDonald's) requires minimal capital and produces high ROIC. A company owned model (Chipotle) requires building every store and needs high unit economics to justify the capital.
quality.FCF Margin: Cash conversion differs dramatically by model. Franchise companies collect royalties with almost no capex; company owned chains must continuously invest in new stores and renovations.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Same store sales growth is the restaurant industry KPI. It measures whether existing locations are attracting more customers and higher check sizes.
quality.EBITDA Margin: Highest weight (30%). Reflects unit level economics: food cost, labor, rent, and other occupancy costs. Strong EBITDA margin means the restaurant model works at the store level.

value.EV/EBITDA: 35% weight. The restaurant industry standard valuation metric.
value.P/FCF: Important for comparing franchise models (high FCF) vs company owned models (lower FCF from store capex).
value.EV/Sales: Low weight because franchise revenue is artificially low (royalty percentage only) while company owned revenue includes full food sales.
value.P/E: Standard earnings multiple.

quality.ROIC: Retailers invest in stores, distribution centers, and inventory. ROIC measures whether those investments produce adequate returns, separating well run retailers from those expanding into unprofitable markets.
quality.Gross Margin: The core retail metric. Costco operates at 13% gross margin with massive volume; Home Depot earns 33% on differentiated home improvement products. Both models work but for completely different reasons.
quality.FCF Margin: Cash after store maintenance, lease obligations, and inventory investment. Growing retailers often generate negative FCF despite positive earnings because expansion costs consume cash.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Comparable store sales and traffic trends. Positive comps mean existing stores are performing; negative comps signal a problem with the assortment, pricing, or customer experience.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard for retail businesses.
value.P/FCF: Lease obligations and store maintenance capex create a gap between EBITDA and cash.
value.EV/Sales: Higher weight because retail margin profiles vary enormously by category and business model.
value.P/E: Standard earnings multiple.

quality.ROIC: Highest weight (35%). Fabs cost $10 to $20 billion to build. Companies making that level of capital commitment must earn exceptional returns. ROIC separates companies with proprietary technology (NVDA, ASML) from those competing in commoditized chips.
quality.Gross Margin: Reflects pricing power and fab utilization. A chip company with 70% gross margin has a technological moat; one at 30% is selling commodity memory or logic at thin margins.
quality.FCF Margin: After spending billions on fab construction and equipment, how much cash actually reaches shareholders? FCF Margin shows the real economic profit after all that capital investment.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Lowest weight (15%) because semiconductors are inherently cyclical. Revenue growth in a chip upcycle is expected and does not necessarily reflect company quality.

value.EV/EBITDA: Capital structure neutral, which matters because fab ownership vs fabless models create very different balance sheets.
value.P/FCF: Critical for fab heavy companies where billions in capex create a massive gap between EBITDA and free cash flow.
value.PEG Ratio: 25% weight. Growth rates vary from negative 20% to positive 100%+ in this sector. PEG normalizes for this extreme dispersion, preventing the fastest grower from always looking expensive.
value.P/E: Low weight because cyclical earnings at the peak of a chip cycle are not sustainable.

quality.ROIC: Software companies invest heavily in R&D and sales to build recurring revenue streams. ROIC measures whether these investments translate into durable returns, separating companies with real product market fit from those buying revenue through unsustainable sales spending.
quality.Gross Margin: Core pricing power metric. SaaS should sustain 70%+ gross margins. Below that, hosting costs, delivery, or support expenses are eroding the inherent scalability of software. Declining gross margin signals commoditization or rising cloud infrastructure costs.
quality.FCF Margin: The honest profitability measure for software. Stock based compensation inflates earnings (it is a non cash expense) but does not affect cash flow. FCF reveals what the business actually generates in cash after all real costs.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Growth is the thesis for most software companies. The market pays premium multiples for growth because recurring subscription revenue compounds. Decelerating growth signals market saturation or competitive pressure.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard but frequently excluded because many growth stage SaaS companies have negative EBITDA from intentional investment in sales and R&D.
value.P/FCF: More reliable than P/E for SaaS because FCF strips out stock compensation distortion. Shows what you actually pay for each dollar of real cash the business generates.
value.EV/Sales: Critical for high growth SaaS where earnings and EBITDA are negative. Revenue multiples are often the only way to compare companies at different stages of profitability.
value.PEG Ratio: Adjusts P/E for growth rate. A company trading at 40x P/E is not expensive if earnings are growing 40% per year. PEG normalizes for the wide growth dispersion in software.

quality.ROIC: Specialty chemical companies invest in proprietary formulations and custom manufacturing. ROIC separates high return specialty producers from low return commodity chemical companies.
quality.Gross Margin: The defining metric. Specialty chemicals earn 40 to 50% gross margins on proprietary products; commodity chemicals earn 15 to 20% competing on price alone. This is the clearest indicator of whether a company has a moat.
quality.FCF Margin: Chemical plants require constant maintenance and environmental compliance spending. FCF Margin shows cash available after these mandatory costs.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Driven by both pricing power (specialty) and volume trends. Consistent revenue growth signals products that customers cannot easily substitute.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard for chemical manufacturers.
value.P/FCF: Plant maintenance capex creates a gap between EBITDA and cash.
value.EV/Sales: Spans a wide margin spectrum from specialty (high margin) to commodity (thin margin).
value.P/E: Standard earnings multiple.

quality.ROIC: Telecoms invest tens of billions in network infrastructure (fiber, cell towers, spectrum) over decades. ROIC shows whether these massive, irreversible investments produce returns above the cost of capital.
quality.FCF Margin: After spending billions every year on network maintenance and expansion, how much cash is actually left? FCF Margin is the reality check on whether the business model works after reinvestment needs.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Driven by subscriber growth and average revenue per user (ARPU). In a mature market, growing revenue means either gaining subscribers from competitors or selling higher value plans.
quality.EBITDA Margin: Highest weight (30%). The telecom industry standard. Depreciation is enormous on network infrastructure, so EBITDA is the common metric for comparing operating performance.

value.EV/EBITDA: Highest weight (40%). The industry standard because EV captures the significant debt used to finance network buildouts.
value.P/FCF: 35% weight. Directly tied to dividend sustainability. Telecom investors buy for yield, and FCF funds those dividends.
value.EV/Sales: Low weight. Revenue is mature and stable in most telecom markets.
value.P/E: Low weight. Heavy depreciation on network infrastructure significantly distorts reported earnings.

quality.ROIC: Separates asset light freight brokers (minimal capital, high ROIC) from heavy carriers operating their own truck fleets (capital intensive, moderate ROIC). The business model choice drives the return profile.
quality.FCF Margin: Truck fleets require constant maintenance and periodic replacement. FCF Margin shows cash available after these non discretionary fleet costs.
quality.Revenue YoY %: Directly tied to freight volumes and pricing (rate per mile). Growing revenue signals either economic strength or market share gains.
quality.EBIT Margin: Operating ratio (100% minus EBIT margin) is the industry KPI for trucking, same as railroads. It measures how many cents of every revenue dollar are consumed by operations.

value.EV/EBITDA: Standard for transportation companies.
value.P/FCF: Fleet capex and equipment replacement create a meaningful gap between EBITDA and actual cash.
value.EV/Sales: Low weight because business model (asset light broker vs asset heavy carrier) dramatically affects margins.
value.P/E: Standard earnings multiple.
